data.text: an unpleasant often strong emotion caused by anticipation or awareness of danger
examples: trembling with fear
a tale that inspired fear = a tale that struck fear into our hearts
a fear of heights
… the preschool years, when fear of the dark is common.
wouldn't speak out for fear of retaliation
were in fear for =afraid of losing their lives
… went into hiding … out of fear for herself and family …
living in fear
fear of God
He was a fighter … who would put the fear of God into opponents. =make opponents very afraid
Starvation is still a real fear for many people around the world.

data.text: to be afraid of: expect with alarm
examples: fear the worst
Don't fear change—embrace it.
There's nothing to fear.
a ruler who was hated and feared
They feared that he was dead. = They feared him (to be) dead. = He was feared (to be) dead.
It was much less difficult than we had feared (it might be).
I fear that we have no choice.
fear God
feared for their lives/safety
Never fear =don't worry—we're here to help.
There's no need to fear; you'll do fine.
Fear not! I will protect you.

data.text: cape on the Atlantic at the mouth of the Cape Fear River in southernmost North Carolina
river 202 miles (325 kilometers) long in central and southeastern North Carolina flowing southeast into the Atlantic

enrichment.etymology: Middle English fer, going back to Old English fǣr, fēr "unexpected danger, peril," going back to Germanic *fēra- or *fēran- (whence also Old Saxon fār "lurking danger," Old High German fāra "ambush, danger," Old Norse fár "evil, mischief, plague"), perhaps going back to a lengthened-grade nominal derivative of a proposed Indo-European verbal base *per- "test, risk"
